title: Star

infobox.value: is a luminous [spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid") of [plasma](/wiki/Plasma_(physics) "Plasma (physics)") held together by [self-gravity](/wiki/Self-gravitation "Self-gravitation").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The [nearest star](/wiki/List_of_nearest_stars_and_brown_dwarfs "List of nearest stars and brown dwarfs") to Earth is the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"). Many other stars are visible to the naked eye at [night](/wiki/Night_sky "Night sky"); their immense distances from Earth make them appear as [fixed](/wiki/Fixed_stars "Fixed stars") points of light. The most prominent stars have been categorised into [constellations](/wiki/Constellation "Constellation") and [asterisms](/wiki/Asterism_(astronomy) "Asterism (astronomy)"), and many of the brightest stars have [proper names](/wiki/Proper_name "Proper name"). [Astronomers](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer") have assembled [star catalogues](/wiki/Star_catalogue "Star catalogue") that identify the known stars and provide standardized [stellar designations](/wiki/Stellar_designation "Stellar designation"). The [observable universe](/wiki/Observable_universe "Observable universe") contains an estimated 1022 to 1024 stars. Only about 4,000 of these stars are visible to the naked eye—all within the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") [galaxy](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Large self-illuminated object in space

This article is about the astronomical object. For other uses, see [Star (disambiguation)](/wiki/Star_(disambiguation) "Star (disambiguation)") and [Stars (disambiguation)](/wiki/Stars_(disambiguation) "Stars (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/The_Sun_in_white_light.jpg/250px-The_Sun_in_white_light.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Sun_in_white_light.jpg)

The [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), a [G-type main-sequence star](/wiki/G-type_main-sequence_star "G-type main-sequence star"), the closest to Earth

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Starsinthesky.jpg/250px-Starsinthesky.jpg)](/wiki/File:Starsinthesky.jpg)

A [star-forming](/wiki/Star_formation#Stellar_nurseries "Star formation") region in the [Large Magellanic Cloud](/wiki/Large_Magellanic_Cloud "Large Magellanic Cloud")

A **star** is a luminous [spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid") of [plasma](/wiki/Plasma_(physics) "Plasma (physics)") held together by [self-gravity](/wiki/Self-gravitation "Self-gravitation").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The [nearest star](/wiki/List_of_nearest_stars_and_brown_dwarfs "List of nearest stars and brown dwarfs") to Earth is the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"). Many other stars are visible to the naked eye at [night](/wiki/Night_sky "Night sky"); their immense distances from Earth make them appear as [fixed](/wiki/Fixed_stars "Fixed stars") points of light. The most prominent stars have been categorised into [constellations](/wiki/Constellation "Constellation") and [asterisms](/wiki/Asterism_(astronomy) "Asterism (astronomy)"), and many of the brightest stars have [proper names](/wiki/Proper_name "Proper name"). [Astronomers](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer") have assembled [star catalogues](/wiki/Star_catalogue "Star catalogue") that identify the known stars and provide standardized [stellar designations](/wiki/Stellar_designation "Stellar designation"). The [observable universe](/wiki/Observable_universe "Observable universe") contains an estimated 1022 to 1024 stars. Only about 4,000 of these stars are visible to the naked eye—all within the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") [galaxy](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

A star's life [begins](/wiki/Star_formation "Star formation") with the [gravitational collapse](/wiki/Gravitational_collapse "Gravitational collapse") of a gaseous [nebula](/wiki/Nebula "Nebula") of material largely comprising [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen"), helium, and traces of heavier elements. Its [total mass](/wiki/Stellar_mass "Stellar mass") mainly determines its [evolution](/wiki/Stellar_evolution "Stellar evolution") and eventual fate. A star shines for [most of its active life](/wiki/Main_sequence "Main sequence") due to the [thermonuclear fusion](/wiki/Thermonuclear_fusion "Thermonuclear fusion") of hydrogen into [helium](/wiki/Helium "Helium") in its core. This process releases energy that traverses the star's interior and [radiates](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation") into [outer space](/wiki/Outer_space "Outer space"). At the end of a star's lifetime, fusion ceases and its core becomes a [stellar remnant](/wiki/Stellar_remnant "Stellar remnant"): a [white dwarf](/wiki/White_dwarf "White dwarf"), a [neutron star](/wiki/Neutron_star "Neutron star"), or—if it is sufficiently massive—a [black hole](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole").

[Stellar nucleosynthesis](/wiki/Stellar_nucleosynthesis "Stellar nucleosynthesis") in stars or their remnants creates almost all naturally occurring [chemical elements](/wiki/Chemical_element "Chemical element") heavier than [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium"). [Stellar mass loss](/wiki/Stellar_mass_loss "Stellar mass loss") or [supernova](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova") explosions return chemically enriched material to the [interstellar medium](/wiki/Interstellar_medium "Interstellar medium"). These elements are then recycled into new stars. Astronomers can determine stellar properties—including mass, age, [metallicity](/wiki/Metallicity "Metallicity") (chemical composition), [variability](/wiki/Variable_star "Variable star"), [distance](/wiki/Distance_(astronomy) "Distance (astronomy)"), and motion through [space](/wiki/Space "Space")—by carrying out observations of a star's [apparent brightness](/wiki/Apparent_magnitude "Apparent magnitude"), [spectrum](/wiki/Astronomical_spectroscopy "Astronomical spectroscopy"), and [changes in its position in the sky](/wiki/Astrometry "Astrometry") over time.

Stars can form orbital systems with other [astronomical objects](/wiki/Astronomical_object "Astronomical object"), as in [planetary systems](/wiki/Planetary_system "Planetary system") and [star systems](/wiki/Star_system "Star system") with [two](/wiki/Binary_star "Binary star") or [more](/wiki/Star_system#Multiple_star_systems "Star system") stars. When two such stars orbit closely, their gravitational interaction can significantly impact their evolution. Stars often form part of much larger gravitationally bound structures, such as [star clusters](/wiki/Star_cluster "Star cluster") and galaxies.

## Etymology

The English word *star* ultimately derives from the [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European "Proto-Indo-European") root \**h₂stḗr*, also meaning 'star' – which is further analyzable as \**h₂eh₁s-* 'to burn' (also the source of the word *ash*) plus \**-tēr* (the [agentive suffix](/wiki/Agentive_suffix "Agentive suffix")). Its [cognates](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") in other languages include Latin *stella*, Greek *aster*, and German *Stern*;[[3]](#cite_note-3) further cognates in English include *[asterisk](/wiki/Asterisk "Asterisk")*, *[asteroid](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid")*, *[astral](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/astral "wikt:astral")*, *[constellation](/wiki/Constellation "Constellation")*, and *[Esther](/wiki/Esther "Esther")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Observation history

See also: [Stars in astrology](/wiki/Stars_in_astrology "Stars in astrology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Dibuix_de_Leo.png/250px-Dibuix_de_Leo.png)](/wiki/File:Dibuix_de_Leo.png)

A 1690 depiction of the constellation of [Leo](/wiki/Leo_(constellation) "Leo (constellation)") the lion by [Johannes Hevelius](/wiki/Johannes_Hevelius "Johannes Hevelius").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Historically, stars have been important to [civilizations](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization") throughout the world. They have been part of religious practices, [divination](/wiki/Divination "Divination") rituals, [mythology](/wiki/Myth "Myth"), used for [celestial navigation](/wiki/Celestial_navigation "Celestial navigation") and orientation, to mark the passage of seasons, and to define calendars.

Early astronomers recognized a difference between "[fixed stars](/wiki/Fixed_stars "Fixed stars")", whose position on the [celestial sphere](/wiki/Celestial_sphere "Celestial sphere") does not change, and "wandering stars" ([planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet")), which move noticeably relative to the fixed stars over days or weeks.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Many ancient astronomers believed that the stars were permanently affixed to a [heavenly sphere](/wiki/Heavenly_sphere "Heavenly sphere") and that they were immutable. By convention, astronomers grouped prominent stars into [asterisms](/wiki/Asterism_(astronomy) "Asterism (astronomy)") and [constellations](/wiki/Constellation "Constellation") and used them to track the motions of the planets and the inferred position of the Sun.[[7]](#cite_note-forbes-7) The motion of the Sun against the background stars (and the horizon) was used to create [calendars](/wiki/Solar_calendar "Solar calendar"), which could be used to regulate agricultural practices.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"), currently used nearly everywhere in the world, is a solar calendar based on the angle of the Earth's rotational axis relative to its local star, the Sun.

The oldest accurately dated [star chart](/wiki/Star_chart "Star chart") was the result of ancient [Egyptian astronomy](/wiki/Egyptian_astronomy "Egyptian astronomy") in 1534 BC.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The [earliest known star catalogues](/wiki/Babylonian_star_catalogues "Babylonian star catalogues") were compiled by the ancient [Babylonian astronomers](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy "Babylonian astronomy") of [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") in the late 2nd millennium BC, during the [Kassite Period](/wiki/Kassites "Kassites") (c. 1531 BC – c. 1155 BC).[[10]](#cite_note-north_1995_30_31-10)

[![Alternative text](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/%D0%A1%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%82_%D0%BE%D1%82_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-%D0%A1%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%82_%D0%BE%D1%82_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A1%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%82_%D0%BE%D1%82_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8_-_panoramio.jpg)

Stars in the night sky

The first [star catalogue](/wiki/Star_catalogue "Star catalogue") in [Greek astronomy](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_astronomy "Ancient Greek astronomy") was created by [Aristillus](/wiki/Aristillus "Aristillus") in approximately 300 BC, with the help of [Timocharis](/wiki/Timocharis "Timocharis").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The star catalog of [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus") (2nd century BC) included 1,020 stars, and was used to assemble [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy")'s star catalogue.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Hipparchus is known for the discovery of the first recorded *[nova](/wiki/Nova "Nova")* (new star).[[13]](#cite_note-13) Many of the constellations and star names in use today derive from Greek astronomy.

Despite the apparent immutability of the heavens, [Chinese astronomers](/wiki/Chinese_astronomy "Chinese astronomy") were aware that new stars could appear.[[14]](#cite_note-clark-14) In 185 AD, they were the first to observe and write about a [supernova](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova"), now known as [SN 185](/wiki/SN_185 "SN 185").[[15]](#cite_note-15) The brightest stellar event in recorded history was the [SN 1006](/wiki/SN_1006 "SN 1006") supernova, which was observed in 1006 and written about by the Egyptian astronomer [Ali ibn Ridwan](/wiki/Ali_ibn_Ridwan "Ali ibn Ridwan") and several Chinese astronomers.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The [SN 1054](/wiki/SN_1054 "SN 1054") supernova, which gave birth to the [Crab Nebula](/wiki/Crab_Nebula "Crab Nebula"), was also observed by Chinese and Islamic astronomers.[[17]](#cite_note-SN1054-17)[[18]](#cite_note-PASP1942-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

[Medieval Islamic astronomers](/wiki/Astronomy_in_the_medieval_Islamic_world "Astronomy in the medieval Islamic world") gave [Arabic names to many stars](/wiki/List_of_Arabic_star_names "List of Arabic star names") that are still used today and they invented numerous [astronomical instruments](/wiki/Astronomy_in_medieval_Islam#Instruments "Astronomy in medieval Islam") that could compute the positions of the stars. They built the first large [observatory](/wiki/Observatory "Observatory") research institutes, mainly to produce *[Zij](/wiki/Zij "Zij")* star catalogues.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Among these, the *[Book of Fixed Stars](/wiki/Book_of_Fixed_Stars "Book of Fixed Stars")* (964) was written by the [Persian](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people") astronomer [Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi](/wiki/Abd_al-Rahman_al-Sufi "Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi"), who observed a number of stars, [star clusters](/wiki/Star_cluster "Star cluster") (including the [Omicron Velorum](/wiki/Omicron_Velorum "Omicron Velorum") and [Brocchi's Clusters](/wiki/Brocchi%27s_Cluster "Brocchi's Cluster")) and [galaxies](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy") (including the [Andromeda Galaxy](/wiki/Andromeda_Galaxy "Andromeda Galaxy")).[[21]](#cite_note-Jones-21) According to A. Zahoor, in the 11th century, the Persian [polymath](/wiki/Polymath "Polymath") scholar [Abu Rayhan Biruni](/wiki/Abu_Rayhan_Biruni "Abu Rayhan Biruni") described the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") galaxy as a multitude of fragments having the properties of [nebulous](/wiki/Nebula "Nebula") stars, and gave the [latitudes](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") of various stars during a [lunar eclipse](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse") in 1019.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

According to Josep Puig, the [Andalusian](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") astronomer [Ibn Bajjah](/wiki/Ibn_Bajjah "Ibn Bajjah") proposed that the Milky Way was made up of many stars that almost touched one another and appeared to be a continuous image due to the effect of [refraction](/wiki/Refraction "Refraction") from sublunary material, citing his observation of the [conjunction](/wiki/Conjunction_(astronomy_and_astrology) "Conjunction (astronomy and astrology)") of Jupiter and Mars on 500 [AH](/wiki/Islamic_calendar "Islamic calendar") (1106/1107 AD) as evidence.[[23]](#cite_note-Montada-23)
Early European astronomers such as [Tycho Brahe](/wiki/Tycho_Brahe "Tycho Brahe") identified new stars in the [night sky](/wiki/Night_sky "Night sky") (later termed *novae*), suggesting that the heavens were not immutable. In 1584, [Giordano Bruno](/wiki/Giordano_Bruno "Giordano Bruno") suggested that the stars were like the Sun, and may have [other planets](/wiki/Extrasolar_planet "Extrasolar planet"), possibly even Earth-like, in orbit around them,[[24]](#cite_note-he_history-24) an idea that had been suggested earlier by the ancient [Greek philosophers](/wiki/Greek_philosophy "Greek philosophy"), [Democritus](/wiki/Democritus "Democritus") and [Epicurus](/wiki/Epicurus "Epicurus"),[[25]](#cite_note-25) and by medieval [Islamic cosmologists](/wiki/Cosmology_in_medieval_Islam "Cosmology in medieval Islam")[[26]](#cite_note-26) such as [Fakhr al-Din al-Razi](/wiki/Fakhr_al-Din_al-Razi "Fakhr al-Din al-Razi").[[27]](#cite_note-Setia-27) By the following century, the idea of the stars being the same as the Sun was reaching a consensus among astronomers. To explain why these stars exerted no net gravitational pull on the Solar System, [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") suggested that the stars were equally distributed in every direction, an idea prompted by the theologian [Richard Bentley](/wiki/Richard_Bentley "Richard Bentley").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The Italian astronomer [Geminiano Montanari](/wiki/Geminiano_Montanari "Geminiano Montanari") recorded observing variations in luminosity of the star [Algol](/wiki/Algol "Algol") in 1667. [Edmond Halley](/wiki/Edmond_Halley "Edmond Halley") published the first measurements of the [proper motion](/wiki/Proper_motion "Proper motion") of a pair of nearby "fixed" stars, demonstrating that they had changed positions since the time of the ancient [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") astronomers Ptolemy and Hipparchus.[[24]](#cite_note-he_history-24)

[William Herschel](/wiki/William_Herschel "William Herschel") was the first astronomer to attempt to determine the distribution of stars in the sky. During the 1780s, he established a series of gauges in 600 directions and counted the stars observed along each line of sight. From this, he deduced that the number of stars steadily increased toward one side of the sky, in the direction of the Milky Way [core](/wiki/Galactic_Center "Galactic Center"). His son [John Herschel](/wiki/John_Herschel "John Herschel") repeated this study in the southern hemisphere and found a corresponding increase in the same direction.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In addition to his other accomplishments, William Herschel is noted for his discovery that some stars do not merely lie along the same line of sight, but are physical companions that form binary star systems.[[30]](#cite_note-Magill1992-30)

The science of [stellar spectroscopy](/wiki/Astronomical_spectroscopy "Astronomical spectroscopy") was pioneered by [Joseph von Fraunhofer](/wiki/Joseph_von_Fraunhofer "Joseph von Fraunhofer") and [Angelo Secchi](/wiki/Angelo_Secchi "Angelo Secchi"). By comparing the spectra of stars such as [Sirius](/wiki/Sirius "Sirius") to the Sun, they found differences in the strength and number of their [absorption lines](/wiki/Spectral_line "Spectral line")—the dark lines in stellar spectra caused by the atmosphere's absorption of specific frequencies. In 1865, Secchi began classifying stars into [spectral types](/wiki/Stellar_classification "Stellar classification").[[31]](#cite_note-31) The modern version of the stellar classification scheme was developed by [Annie J. Cannon](/wiki/Annie_Jump_Cannon "Annie Jump Cannon") during the early 1900s.[[32]](#cite_note-HubenyMihalas2014-32)

The first direct measurement of the distance to a star ([61 Cygni](/wiki/61_Cygni "61 Cygni") at 11.4 [light-years](/wiki/Light-years "Light-years")) was made in 1838 by [Friedrich Bessel](/wiki/Friedrich_Bessel "Friedrich Bessel") using the [parallax](/wiki/Parallax "Parallax") technique. Parallax measurements demonstrated the vast separation of the stars in the heavens.[[24]](#cite_note-he_history-24) Observation of double stars gained increasing importance during the 19th century. In 1834, Friedrich Bessel observed changes in the proper motion of the star Sirius and inferred a hidden companion. [Edward Pickering](/wiki/Edward_Charles_Pickering "Edward Charles Pickering") discovered the first [spectroscopic binary](/wiki/Spectroscopic_binary "Spectroscopic binary") in 1899 when he observed the periodic splitting of the spectral lines of the star [Mizar](/wiki/Mizar_(star) "Mizar (star)") in a 104-day period. Detailed observations of many binary star systems were collected by astronomers such as [Friedrich Georg Wilhelm von Struve](/wiki/Friedrich_Georg_Wilhelm_von_Struve "Friedrich Georg Wilhelm von Struve") and [S. W. Burnham](/wiki/Sherburne_Wesley_Burnham "Sherburne Wesley Burnham"), allowing the masses of stars to be determined from computation of [orbital elements](/wiki/Orbital_elements "Orbital elements"). The first solution to the problem of deriving an orbit of binary stars from telescope observations was made by Felix Savary in 1827.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The twentieth century saw increasingly rapid advances in the scientific study of stars. The photograph became a valuable astronomical tool. [Karl Schwarzschild](/wiki/Karl_Schwarzschild "Karl Schwarzschild") discovered that the color of a star and, hence, its temperature, could be determined by comparing the [visual magnitude](/wiki/Visual_magnitude "Visual magnitude") against the [photographic magnitude](/wiki/Photographic_magnitude "Photographic magnitude"). The development of the [photoelectric](/wiki/Photoelectric_effect "Photoelectric effect") [photometer](/wiki/Photometer "Photometer") allowed precise measurements of magnitude at multiple wavelength intervals. In 1921 [Albert A. Michelson](/wiki/Albert_A._Michelson "Albert A. Michelson") made the first measurements of a stellar diameter using an [interferometer](/wiki/Interferometer "Interferometer") on the [Hooker telescope](/wiki/100-inch_Hooker_telescope "100-inch Hooker telescope") at [Mount Wilson Observatory](/wiki/Mount_Wilson_Observatory "Mount Wilson Observatory").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Important theoretical work on the physical structure of stars occurred during the first decades of the twentieth century. In 1913, the [Hertzsprung–Russell diagram](/wiki/Hertzsprung%E2%80%93Russell_diagram "Hertzsprung–Russell diagram") was developed, propelling the astrophysical study of stars. Successful [models](/wiki/Stellar_model "Stellar model") were developed to explain the interiors of stars and stellar evolution. [Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin](/wiki/Cecilia_Payne-Gaposchkin "Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin") first proposed that stars were made primarily of hydrogen and helium in her 1925 PhD thesis.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The spectra of stars were further understood through advances in [quantum physics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics"). This allowed the chemical composition of the stellar atmosphere to be determined.[[36]](#cite_note-new_cosmos-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Milky_Way_IR_Spitzer.jpg/250px-Milky_Way_IR_Spitzer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milky_Way_IR_Spitzer.jpg)

[Spitzer Space Telescope](/wiki/Spitzer_Space_Telescope "Spitzer Space Telescope") infrared image showing a multitude of stars in the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") galaxy

With the exception of rare events such as supernovae and [supernova impostors](/wiki/Supernova_impostor "Supernova impostor"), individual stars have primarily been observed in the [Local Group](/wiki/Local_Group "Local Group"),[[37]](#cite_note-gordon2016-37) and especially in the visible part of the Milky Way (as demonstrated by the detailed star catalogues available for the Milky Way galaxy) and its satellites.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Individual stars such as Cepheid variables have been observed in the [M87](/wiki/Messier_87 "Messier 87")[[39]](#cite_note-39) and [M100](/wiki/Messier_100 "Messier 100") galaxies of the [Virgo Cluster](/wiki/Virgo_Cluster "Virgo Cluster"),[[40]](#cite_note-40) as well as luminous stars in some other relatively nearby galaxies.[[41]](#cite_note-41) With the aid of [gravitational lensing](/wiki/Gravitational_lensing "Gravitational lensing"), a single star (named [Icarus](/wiki/Icarus_(star) "Icarus (star)")) has been observed at 9 billion light-years away.[[42]](#cite_note-NA-20180402-42)[[43]](#cite_note-SPC-20180402-43)

## Designations

Main articles: [Stellar designation](/wiki/Stellar_designation "Stellar designation"), [Astronomical naming conventions](/wiki/Astronomical_naming_conventions "Astronomical naming conventions"), and [Star catalogue](/wiki/Star_catalogue "Star catalogue")

The concept of a constellation was known to exist during the [Babylonian](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon") period. Ancient sky watchers imagined that prominent arrangements of stars formed patterns, and they associated these with particular aspects of nature or their myths. Twelve of these formations lay along the band of the [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic") and these became the basis of [astrology](/wiki/Astrology "Astrology").[[44]](#cite_note-koch95-44) Many of the more prominent individual stars were given names, particularly with [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") or [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") designations.

As well as certain constellations and the Sun itself, individual stars have their own [myths](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology").[[45]](#cite_note-mythology-45) To the [Ancient Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_religion "Ancient Greek religion"), some "stars", known as [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet") (Greek πλανήτης (planētēs), meaning "wanderer"), represented various important deities, from which the names of the planets [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)"), [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus"), [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"), [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter") and [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn") were taken.[[45]](#cite_note-mythology-45) ([Uranus](/wiki/Uranus "Uranus") and [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune") were [Greek](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology") and [Roman gods](/wiki/Roman_mythology "Roman mythology"), but neither planet was known in Antiquity because of their low brightness. Their names were assigned by later astronomers.)

Circa 1600, the names of the constellations were used to name the stars in the corresponding regions of the sky. The German astronomer [Johann Bayer](/wiki/Johann_Bayer "Johann Bayer") created a series of star maps and applied Greek letters as [designations](/wiki/Bayer_designation "Bayer designation") to the stars in each constellation. Later a numbering system based on the star's [right ascension](/wiki/Right_ascension "Right ascension") was invented and added to [John Flamsteed](/wiki/John_Flamsteed "John Flamsteed")'s star catalogue in his book *"Historia coelestis Britannica"* (the 1712 edition), whereby this numbering system came to be called *[Flamsteed designation](/wiki/Flamsteed_designation "Flamsteed designation")* or *Flamsteed numbering*.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The internationally recognized authority for naming celestial bodies is the [International Astronomical Union](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union") (IAU).[[48]](#cite_note-space_law09-48) The International Astronomical Union maintains the [Working Group on Star Names](/wiki/IAU_Working_Group_on_Star_Names "IAU Working Group on Star Names") (WGSN)[[49]](#cite_note-WGSN-49) which catalogs and standardizes proper names for stars.[[50]](#cite_note-50) A number of private companies sell names of stars which are not recognized by the IAU, professional astronomers, or the amateur astronomy community.[[51]](#cite_note-andersen10-51) The [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library") calls this an [unregulated](/wiki/Regulation "Regulation") [commercial enterprise](/wiki/Commercial_enterprise "Commercial enterprise"),[[52]](#cite_note-astrometry05-52)[[53]](#cite_note-bl_disclaimer-53) and the [New York City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Consumer_and_Worker_Protection "New York City Department of Consumer and Worker Protection") issued a violation against one such star-naming company for engaging in a deceptive trade practice.[[54]](#cite_note-pliat02-54)[[55]](#cite_note-sclafani19980508-55)

## Units of measurement

Although stellar parameters can be expressed in [SI units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") or [Gaussian units](/wiki/Gaussian_units "Gaussian units"), it is often most convenient to express [mass](/wiki/Mass "Mass"), [luminosity](/wiki/Luminosity "Luminosity"), and [radii](/wiki/Radius "Radius") in solar units, based on the characteristics of the Sun. In 2015, the IAU defined a set of *nominal* solar values (defined as SI constants, without uncertainties) which can be used for quoting stellar parameters:

:   |  |  |
    | --- | --- |
    | [nominal solar luminosity](/wiki/Solar_luminosity "Solar luminosity") | *L☉* = 3.828×1026 W[[56]](#cite_note-Prsa16-56) |
    | [nominal solar radius](/wiki/Solar_radius "Solar radius") | *R☉* = 6.957×108 m[[56]](#cite_note-Prsa16-56) |

The [solar mass](/wiki/Solar_mass "Solar mass") M☉ was not explicitly defined by the IAU due to the large relative uncertainty (10−4) of the [Newtonian constant of gravitation](/wiki/Newtonian_constant_of_gravitation "Newtonian constant of gravitation") *G*. Since the product of the Newtonian constant of gravitation and solar mass
together (*G*M☉) has been determined to much greater precision, the IAU defined the *nominal* solar mass parameter to be:

:   |  |  |
    | --- | --- |
    | nominal solar mass parameter: | *GM☉* = 1.3271244×1020 m3/s2[[56]](#cite_note-Prsa16-56) |

The nominal solar mass parameter can be combined with the most recent (2014) CODATA estimate of the Newtonian constant of gravitation *G* to derive the solar mass to be approximately 1.9885×1030 kg. Although the exact values for the luminosity, radius, mass parameter, and mass may vary slightly in the future due to observational uncertainties, the 2015 IAU nominal constants will remain the same SI values as they remain useful measures for quoting stellar parameters.

Large lengths, such as the radius of a giant star or the [semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_and_semi-minor_axes "Semi-major and semi-minor axes") of a binary star system, are often expressed in terms of the [astronomical unit](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit")—approximately equal to the mean distance between the Earth and the Sun (150 million km or approximately 93 million miles). In 2012, the IAU defined the [astronomical constant](/wiki/Astronomical_constant "Astronomical constant") to be an exact length in meters: 149,597,870,700 m.[[56]](#cite_note-Prsa16-56)

## Formation and evolution

Main article: [Stellar evolution](/wiki/Stellar_evolution "Stellar evolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Star_life_cycles_red_dwarf_en.svg/500px-Star_life_cycles_red_dwarf_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Star_life_cycles_red_dwarf_en.svg)

Stellar evolution of low-mass (left cycle) and high-mass (right cycle) stars, with examples in italics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/A_Comparison_of_Star_Sizes.jpg/500px-A_Comparison_of_Star_Sizes.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Comparison_of_Star_Sizes.jpg)

Size comparison (radius and mass) of a [red dwarf](/wiki/Red_dwarf "Red dwarf"), the Sun, a supermassive [blue supergiant](/wiki/Blue_supergiant "Blue supergiant"), and a [red giant](/wiki/Red_giant "Red giant")

Stars condense from regions of [space](/wiki/Interstellar_medium "Interstellar medium") of higher matter density, yet those regions are less dense than within a [vacuum chamber](/wiki/Vacuum_chamber "Vacuum chamber"). These regions—known as *[molecular clouds](/wiki/Molecular_cloud "Molecular cloud")*—consist mostly of hydrogen, with about 23 to 28 percent helium and a few percent heavier elements. One example of such a star-forming region is the [Orion Nebula](/wiki/Orion_Nebula "Orion Nebula").[[57]](#cite_note-57) Most stars form in groups of dozens to hundreds of thousands of stars.[[58]](#cite_note-58) [Massive stars](/wiki/O-type_star "O-type star") in these groups may powerfully illuminate those clouds, [ionizing](/wiki/Ion "Ion") the hydrogen, and creating [H II regions](/wiki/H_II_region "H II region"). Such feedback effects, from star formation, may ultimately disrupt the cloud and prevent further star formation.[[59]](#cite_note-59)
All stars spend the majority of their existence as *[main-sequence](/wiki/Main_sequence "Main sequence")* stars, fueled primarily by the nuclear fusion of hydrogen into helium within their cores. However, stars of different masses have markedly different properties at various stages of their development. The ultimate fate of more massive stars differs from that of less massive stars, as do their luminosities and the impact they have on their environment. Accordingly, astronomers often group stars by their mass:[[60]](#cite_note-60)

* *Very low mass stars*, with masses below 0.5 M☉, are fully convective and distribute [helium](/wiki/Helium "Helium") evenly throughout the whole star while on the main sequence. Therefore, they never undergo shell burning and never become [red giants](/wiki/Red_giant "Red giant"). After exhausting their hydrogen they become [helium white dwarfs](/wiki/White_dwarf#Stars_with_very_low_mass "White dwarf") and slowly cool.[[61]](#cite_note-adams-61) As the lifetime of 0.5 M☉ stars is longer than the [age of the universe](/wiki/Age_of_the_universe "Age of the universe"), no such star has yet reached the white dwarf stage.
* *Low mass stars* (including the Sun), with a mass between 0.5 M☉ and ~2.25 M☉ depending on composition, do become red giants as their core hydrogen is depleted and they begin to burn helium in core in a [helium flash](/wiki/Helium_flash "Helium flash"); they develop a degenerate carbon-oxygen core later on the [asymptotic giant branch](/wiki/Asymptotic_giant_branch "Asymptotic giant branch"); they finally blow off their outer shell as a [planetary nebula](/wiki/Planetary_nebula "Planetary nebula") and leave behind their core in the form of a white dwarf.[[62]](#cite_note-Kolb2014-62)[[63]](#cite_note-Bisnovatyi-Kogan2013-63)
* *Intermediate-mass stars*, between ~2.25 M☉ and ~8 M☉, pass through evolutionary stages similar to low mass stars, but after a relatively short period on the [red-giant branch](/wiki/Red-giant_branch "Red-giant branch") they ignite helium without a flash and spend an extended period in the [red clump](/wiki/Red_clump "Red clump") before forming a degenerate carbon-oxygen core.[[62]](#cite_note-Kolb2014-62)[[63]](#cite_note-Bisnovatyi-Kogan2013-63)
* *Massive stars* generally have a minimum mass of ~8 M☉.[[64]](#cite_note-64) After exhausting the hydrogen at the core these stars become [supergiants](/wiki/Supergiant "Supergiant") and go on to [fuse](/wiki/Nuclear_fusion "Nuclear fusion") elements heavier than helium. Many end their lives when their cores collapse and they explode as supernovae.[[62]](#cite_note-Kolb2014-62)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Star formation

Main article: [Star formation](/wiki/Star_formation "Star formation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Witness_the_Birth_of_a_Star.jpg/250px-Witness_the_Birth_of_a_Star.jpg)](/wiki/File:Witness_the_Birth_of_a_Star.jpg)

Artist's conception of the birth of a star within a dense [molecular cloud](/wiki/Molecular_cloud "Molecular cloud")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/W40_star-forming_region.jpg/250px-W40_star-forming_region.jpg)](/wiki/File:W40_star-forming_region.jpg)

A cluster of approximately 500 young stars lies within the nearby [W40](/wiki/Westerhout_40 "Westerhout 40") stellar nursery.

The formation of a star begins with gravitational instability within a molecular cloud, caused by regions of higher density—often triggered by compression of clouds by radiation from massive stars, expanding bubbles in the interstellar medium, the collision of different molecular clouds, or the [collision of galaxies](/wiki/Interacting_galaxy "Interacting galaxy") (as in a [starburst galaxy](/wiki/Starburst_galaxy "Starburst galaxy")).[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) When a region reaches a sufficient density of matter to satisfy the criteria for [Jeans instability](/wiki/Jeans_instability "Jeans instability"), it begins to collapse under its own gravitational force.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

As the cloud collapses, individual conglomerations of dense dust and gas form "[Bok globules](/wiki/Bok_globule "Bok globule")". As a globule collapses and the density increases, the gravitational energy converts into heat and the temperature rises. When the protostellar cloud has approximately reached the stable condition of [hydrostatic equilibrium](/wiki/Hydrostatic_equilibrium "Hydrostatic equilibrium"), a [protostar](/wiki/Protostar "Protostar") forms at the core.[[69]](#cite_note-69) These [pre-main-sequence stars](/wiki/Pre-main-sequence_star "Pre-main-sequence star") are often surrounded by a [protoplanetary disk](/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk "Protoplanetary disk") and powered mainly by the conversion of gravitational energy. The period of gravitational contraction lasts about 10 million years for a star like the Sun, up to 100 million years for a red dwarf.[[70]](#cite_note-Hanslmeier2010-70)

Early stars of less than 2 M☉ are called [T Tauri stars](/wiki/T_Tauri_star "T Tauri star"), while those with greater mass are [Herbig Ae/Be stars](/wiki/Herbig_Ae/Be_star "Herbig Ae/Be star"). These newly formed stars emit jets of gas along their axis of rotation, which may reduce the [angular momentum](/wiki/Angular_momentum "Angular momentum") of the collapsing star and result in small patches of nebulosity known as [Herbig–Haro objects](/wiki/Herbig%E2%80%93Haro_object "Herbig–Haro object").[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-smith04-72)
These jets, in combination with radiation from nearby massive stars, may help to drive away the surrounding cloud from which the star was formed.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Early in their development, T Tauri stars follow the [Hayashi track](/wiki/Hayashi_track "Hayashi track")—they contract and decrease in luminosity while remaining at roughly the same temperature. Less massive T Tauri stars follow this track to the main sequence, while more massive stars turn onto the [Henyey track](/wiki/Henyey_track "Henyey track").[[74]](#cite_note-Darling2004-74)

Most stars are observed to be members of binary star systems, and the properties of those binaries are the result of the conditions in which they formed.[[75]](#cite_note-75) A gas cloud must lose its angular momentum in order to collapse and form a star. The fragmentation of the cloud into multiple stars distributes some of that angular momentum. The primordial binaries transfer some angular momentum by gravitational interactions during close encounters with other stars in young stellar clusters. These interactions tend to split apart more widely separated (soft) binaries while causing hard binaries to become more tightly bound. This produces the separation of binaries into their two observed populations distributions.[[76]](#cite_note-Padmanabhan2000-76)

### Main sequence

Main article: [Main sequence](/wiki/Main_sequence "Main sequence")

Stars spend about 90% of their lifetimes fusing hydrogen into helium in high-temperature-and-pressure reactions in their cores. Such stars are said to be on the main sequence and are called dwarf stars. Starting at zero-age main sequence, the proportion of helium in a star's core will steadily increase, the rate of nuclear fusion at the core will slowly increase, as will the star's temperature and luminosity.[[77]](#cite_note-77)
The Sun, for example, is estimated to have increased in luminosity by about 40% since it reached the main sequence 4.6 billion (4.6×109) years ago.[[78]](#cite_note-sun_future-78)

Every star generates a [stellar wind](/wiki/Stellar_wind "Stellar wind") of particles that causes a continual outflow of gas into space. For most stars, the mass lost is negligible. The Sun loses 10−14 *M*☉ every year,[[79]](#cite_note-79) or about 0.01% of its total mass over its entire lifespan. However, very massive stars can lose 10−7 to 10−5 *M*☉ each year, significantly affecting their evolution.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Stars that begin with more than 50 M☉ can lose over half their total mass while on the main sequence.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/H-R_diagram_-edited-3.gif/500px-H-R_diagram_-edited-3.gif)](/wiki/File:H-R_diagram_-edited-3.gif)

An example of a [Hertzsprung–Russell diagram](/wiki/Hertzsprung%E2%80%93Russell_diagram "Hertzsprung–Russell diagram") for a set of stars that includes the Sun (center) (see [Classification](#Classification))

The time a star spends on the main sequence depends primarily on the amount of fuel it has and the rate at which it fuses it. The Sun is expected to live 10 billion (1010) years. Massive stars consume their fuel very rapidly and are short-lived. Low mass stars consume their fuel very slowly. Stars less massive than 0.25 M☉, called [red dwarfs](/wiki/Red_dwarf "Red dwarf"), are able to fuse nearly all of their mass while stars of about 1 M☉ can only fuse about 10% of their mass. The combination of their slow fuel-consumption and relatively large usable fuel supply allows low mass stars to last about one trillion (10×1012) years; the most extreme of 0.08 M☉ will last for about 12 trillion years. Red dwarfs become [hotter and more luminous](/wiki/Blue_dwarf_(red-dwarf_stage) "Blue dwarf (red-dwarf stage)") as they accumulate helium. When they eventually run out of hydrogen, they contract into a white dwarf and decline in temperature.[[61]](#cite_note-adams-61) Since the lifespan of such stars is greater than the current age of the universe (13.8 billion years), no stars under about 0.85 M☉[[82]](#cite_note-saomainseq-82) are expected to have moved off the main sequence.

Besides mass, the elements heavier than helium can play a significant role in the evolution of stars. Astronomers label all elements heavier than helium "metals", and call the chemical [concentration](/wiki/Concentration "Concentration") of these elements in a star, its [metallicity](/wiki/Metallicity "Metallicity"). A star's metallicity can influence the time the star takes to burn its fuel, and controls the formation of its magnetic fields,[[83]](#cite_note-83) which affects the strength of its stellar wind.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Older, [population II](/wiki/Stellar_population "Stellar population") stars have substantially less metallicity than the younger, population I stars due to the composition of the molecular clouds from which they formed. Over time, such clouds become increasingly enriched in heavier elements as older stars die and shed portions of their [atmospheres](/wiki/Stellar_atmosphere "Stellar atmosphere").[[85]](#cite_note-Astrophysics1984-85)

### Post–main sequence

Main articles: [Subgiant](/wiki/Subgiant "Subgiant"), [Red giant](/wiki/Red_giant "Red giant"), [Horizontal branch](/wiki/Horizontal_branch "Horizontal branch"), [Red clump](/wiki/Red_clump "Red clump"), and [Asymptotic giant branch](/wiki/Asymptotic_giant_branch "Asymptotic giant branch")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Betelgeuse_captured_by_ALMA.jpg/250px-Betelgeuse_captured_by_ALMA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Betelgeuse_captured_by_ALMA.jpg)

[Betelgeuse](/wiki/Betelgeuse "Betelgeuse") as seen by [ALMA](/wiki/Atacama_Large_Millimeter_Array "Atacama Large Millimeter Array"). This is the first time that ALMA has observed the surface of a star and resulted in the highest-resolution image of Betelgeuse available.

As stars of at least 0.4 M☉[[86]](#cite_note-late_stages-86) exhaust the supply of hydrogen at their core, they start to fuse hydrogen in a shell surrounding the helium core. The outer layers of the star expand and cool greatly as they transition into a [red giant](/wiki/Red_giant "Red giant"). In some cases, they will fuse heavier [elements](/wiki/Chemical_element "Chemical element") at the core or in shells around the core. As the stars expand, they throw part of their mass, enriched with those heavier elements, into the interstellar environment, to be recycled later as new stars.[[87]](#cite_note-87) In about 5 billion years, when the Sun enters the helium burning phase, it will expand to a maximum radius of roughly 1 astronomical unit (150 million kilometres), 250 times its present size, and lose 30% of its current mass.[[78]](#cite_note-sun_future-78)[[88]](#cite_note-sun_future_schroder-88)

As the hydrogen-burning shell produces more helium, the core increases in mass and temperature. In a red giant of up to 2.25 M☉, the mass of the helium core becomes degenerate prior to [helium fusion](/wiki/Helium_fusion "Helium fusion"). Finally, when the temperature increases sufficiently, core helium fusion begins explosively in what is called a [helium flash](/wiki/Helium_flash "Helium flash"), and the star rapidly shrinks in radius, increases its surface temperature, and moves to the [horizontal branch](/wiki/Horizontal_branch "Horizontal branch") of the HR diagram. For more massive stars, helium core fusion starts before the core becomes degenerate, and the star spends some time in the [red clump](/wiki/Red_clump "Red clump"), slowly burning helium, before the outer convective envelope collapses and the star then moves to the horizontal branch.[[89]](#cite_note-iben-89)

After a star has fused the helium of its core, it begins fusing helium along a shell surrounding the hot carbon core. The star then follows an evolutionary path called the [asymptotic giant branch](/wiki/Asymptotic_giant_branch "Asymptotic giant branch") (AGB) that parallels the other described red-giant phase, but with a higher luminosity. The more massive AGB stars may undergo a brief period of carbon fusion before the core becomes degenerate. During the AGB phase, stars undergo [thermal pulses](/wiki/Thermal_pulse "Thermal pulse") due to instabilities in the core of the star. In these thermal pulses, the luminosity of the star [varies](/wiki/Variable_star "Variable star") and matter is ejected from the star's atmosphere, ultimately forming a planetary nebula. As much as 50 to 70% of a star's mass can be ejected in this [mass loss](/wiki/Stellar_mass_loss "Stellar mass loss") process. Because energy transport in an AGB star is primarily by [convection](/wiki/Convection "Convection"), this ejected material is enriched with the fusion products [dredged up](/wiki/Dredge-up "Dredge-up") from the core. Therefore, the planetary nebula is enriched with elements like carbon and oxygen. Ultimately, the planetary nebula disperses, enriching the general interstellar medium.[[90]](#cite_note-carroll_ostlie_ch13-90) Therefore, future generations of stars are made of the "star stuff" from past stars.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

#### Massive stars

Main articles: [Supergiant star](/wiki/Supergiant_star "Supergiant star"), [Hypergiant](/wiki/Hypergiant "Hypergiant"), and [Wolf–Rayet star](/wiki/Wolf%E2%80%93Rayet_star "Wolf–Rayet star")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Layers_of_an_evolved_star.png/500px-Layers_of_an_evolved_star.png)](/wiki/File:Layers_of_an_evolved_star.png)

Onion-like layers at the core of a massive, evolved star just before core collapse

During their helium-burning phase, a star of more than 9 solar masses expands to form first a [blue supergiant](/wiki/Blue_supergiant "Blue supergiant") and then a [red supergiant](/wiki/Red_supergiant "Red supergiant"). Particularly massive stars (exceeding 40 solar masses, like [Alnilam](/wiki/Alnilam "Alnilam"), the central blue supergiant of [Orion's Belt](/wiki/Orion%27s_Belt "Orion's Belt"))[[92]](#cite_note-92) do not become red supergiants due to high mass loss.[[93]](#cite_note-93) These may instead evolve to a [Wolf–Rayet star](/wiki/Wolf%E2%80%93Rayet_star "Wolf–Rayet star"), characterised by spectra dominated by emission lines of elements heavier than hydrogen, which have reached the surface due to strong convection and intense mass loss, or from stripping of the outer layers.[[94]](#cite_note-ContiLoore2012-94)

When helium is exhausted at the core of a massive star, the core contracts and the temperature and pressure rises enough to fuse [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") (see [Carbon-burning process](/wiki/Carbon-burning_process "Carbon-burning process")). This process continues, with the successive stages being fueled by [neon](/wiki/Neon "Neon") (see [neon-burning process](/wiki/Neon-burning_process "Neon-burning process")), [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") (see [oxygen-burning process](/wiki/Oxygen-burning_process "Oxygen-burning process")), and [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon") (see [silicon-burning process](/wiki/Silicon-burning_process "Silicon-burning process")). Near the end of the star's life, fusion continues along a series of onion-layer shells within a massive star. Each shell fuses a different element, with the outermost shell fusing hydrogen; the next shell fusing helium, and so forth.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

The final stage occurs when a massive star begins producing iron. Since iron nuclei are more [tightly bound](/wiki/Binding_energy "Binding energy") than any heavier nuclei, any fusion beyond iron does not produce a net release of energy.[[96]](#cite_note-sneden-96)

Some massive stars, particularly [luminous blue variables](/wiki/Luminous_blue_variable "Luminous blue variable"), are very unstable to the extent that they violently shed their mass into space in events known as [supernova impostors](/wiki/Supernova_impostor "Supernova impostor"), becoming significantly brighter in the process. [Eta Carinae](/wiki/Eta_Carinae "Eta Carinae") is known for having undergone a supernova impostor event, the Great Eruption, in the 19th century.

#### Collapse

As a star's core shrinks, the intensity of radiation from that surface increases, creating such [radiation pressure](/wiki/Radiation_pressure "Radiation pressure") on the outer shell of gas that it will push those layers away, forming a planetary nebula. If what remains after the outer atmosphere has been shed is less than roughly 1.4 M☉, it shrinks to a relatively tiny object about the size of Earth, known as a [white dwarf](/wiki/White_dwarf "White dwarf"). White dwarfs lack the mass for further gravitational compression to take place.[[97]](#cite_note-97) The [electron-degenerate matter](/wiki/Electron-degenerate_matter "Electron-degenerate matter") inside a white dwarf is no longer a plasma. Eventually, white dwarfs fade into [black dwarfs](/wiki/Black_dwarf "Black dwarf") over a very long period of time.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Crab_Nebula.jpg/250px-Crab_Nebula.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crab_Nebula.jpg)

The [Crab Nebula](/wiki/Crab_Nebula "Crab Nebula"), remnants of a supernova that was first observed around 1050 AD

In massive stars, fusion continues until the iron core has grown so large (more than 1.4 M☉) that it can no longer support its own mass. This core will suddenly collapse as its electrons are driven into its protons, forming neutrons, [neutrinos](/wiki/Neutrino "Neutrino"), and gamma rays in a burst of [electron capture](/wiki/Electron_capture "Electron capture") and [inverse beta decay](/wiki/Inverse_beta_decay "Inverse beta decay"). The [shockwave](/wiki/Shock_wave "Shock wave") formed by this sudden collapse causes the rest of the star to explode in a supernova. Supernovae become so bright that they may briefly outshine the star's entire home galaxy. When they occur within the Milky Way, supernovae have historically been observed by naked-eye observers as "new stars" where none seemingly existed before.[[99]](#cite_note-supernova-99)

A supernova explosion blows away the star's outer layers, leaving a [remnant](/wiki/Supernova_remnant "Supernova remnant") such as the Crab Nebula.[[99]](#cite_note-supernova-99) The core is compressed into a [neutron star](/wiki/Neutron_star "Neutron star"), which sometimes manifests itself as a [pulsar](/wiki/Pulsar "Pulsar") or [X-ray burster](/wiki/X-ray_burster "X-ray burster"). In the case of the largest stars, the remnant is a black hole greater than 4 M☉.[[100]](#cite_note-100) In a neutron star the matter is in a state known as [neutron-degenerate matter](/wiki/Neutron-degenerate_matter "Neutron-degenerate matter"), with a more exotic form of degenerate matter, [QCD matter](/wiki/QCD_matter "QCD matter"), possibly present in the core.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

The blown-off outer layers of dying stars include heavy elements, which may be recycled during the formation of new stars. These heavy elements allow the formation of rocky planets. The outflow from supernovae and the stellar wind of large stars play an important part in shaping the interstellar medium.[[99]](#cite_note-supernova-99)

#### Binary stars

[Binary stars](/wiki/Binary_stars "Binary stars")' evolution may significantly differ from that of single stars of the same mass. For example, when any star expands to become a red giant, it may overflow its [Roche lobe](/wiki/Roche_lobe "Roche lobe"), the surrounding region where material is gravitationally bound to it; if stars in a binary system are close enough, some of that material may overflow to the other star, yielding phenomena including [contact binaries](/wiki/Contact_binaries "Contact binaries"), [common-envelope](/wiki/Common_envelope "Common envelope") binaries, [cataclysmic variables](/wiki/Cataclysmic_variable_star "Cataclysmic variable star"), [blue stragglers](/wiki/Blue_stragglers "Blue stragglers"),[[102]](#cite_note-102) and [Type Ia supernovae](/wiki/Type_Ia_supernova "Type Ia supernova"). Mass transfer leads to cases such as the [Algol paradox](/wiki/Algol_paradox "Algol paradox"), where the most-evolved star in a system is the least massive.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

The evolution of binary star and higher-order [star systems](/wiki/Star_system "Star system") is intensely researched since so many stars have been found to be members of binary systems. Around half of Sun-like stars, and an even higher proportion of more massive stars, form in multiple systems, and this may greatly influence such phenomena as novae and supernovae, the formation of certain types of star, and the enrichment of space with nucleosynthesis products.[[104]](#cite_note-beccari2019-104)

The influence of binary star evolution on the formation of evolved massive stars such as [luminous blue variables](/wiki/Luminous_blue_variable "Luminous blue variable"), Wolf–Rayet stars, and the progenitors of certain classes of [core collapse supernova](/wiki/Core_collapse_supernova "Core collapse supernova") is still disputed. Single massive stars may be unable to expel their outer layers fast enough to form the types and numbers of evolved stars that are observed, or to produce progenitors that would explode as the supernovae that are observed. Mass transfer through gravitational stripping in binary systems is seen by some astronomers as the solution to that problem.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)

## Distribution

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Sirius_A_and_B_artwork.jpg/250px-Sirius_A_and_B_artwork.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sirius_A_and_B_artwork.jpg)

Artist's impression of the [Sirius](/wiki/Sirius "Sirius") system, a [white dwarf](/wiki/White_dwarf "White dwarf") star in orbit around an [A-type main-sequence star](/wiki/A-type_main-sequence_star "A-type main-sequence star")

Stars are not spread uniformly across the universe but are normally grouped into galaxies along with interstellar gas and dust. A typical large galaxy like the Milky Way contains hundreds of billions of stars. There are more than 2 trillion (1012) galaxies, though most are less than 10% the mass of the Milky Way.[[108]](#cite_note-NYT-20161017-108) Overall, there are likely to be between 1022 and 1024 stars,[[109]](#cite_note-ESA-2019-109)[[110]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-110) which are more stars than all the [grains of sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand") on planet Earth.[[111]](#cite_note-SU-20020201-111)[[112]](#cite_note-Borenstein2010-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113) Most stars are within galaxies, but between 10 and 50% of the starlight in large [galaxy clusters](/wiki/Galaxy_cluster "Galaxy cluster") may come from stars outside of any galaxy.[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116)

A multi-star system consists of two or more gravitationally bound stars that [orbit each other](/wiki/Stellar_dynamics "Stellar dynamics"). The simplest and most common multi-star system is a binary star, but systems of three or more stars exist. For reasons of orbital stability, such multi-star systems are often organized into hierarchical sets of binary stars.[[117]](#cite_note-117) Larger groups are called star clusters. These range from loose [stellar associations](/wiki/Stellar_kinematics "Stellar kinematics") with only a few stars to [open clusters](/wiki/Open_cluster "Open cluster") with dozens to thousands of stars, up to enormous [globular clusters](/wiki/Globular_cluster "Globular cluster") with hundreds of thousands of stars. Such systems orbit their host galaxy. The stars in an open or globular cluster all formed from the same [giant molecular cloud](/wiki/Giant_molecular_cloud "Giant molecular cloud"), so all members normally have similar ages and compositions.[[90]](#cite_note-carroll_ostlie_ch13-90)

Many stars are observed, and most or all may have originally formed in gravitationally bound, multiple-star systems. This is particularly true for very massive O and B class stars, 80% of which are believed to be part of multiple-star systems. The proportion of single star systems increases with decreasing star mass, so that only 25% of red dwarfs are known to have stellar companions. As 85% of all stars are red dwarfs, more than two thirds of stars in the Milky Way are likely single red dwarfs.[[118]](#cite_note-118) In a 2017 study of the [Perseus molecular cloud](/wiki/Perseus_molecular_cloud "Perseus molecular cloud"), astronomers found that most of the newly formed stars are in binary systems. In the model that best explained the data, all stars initially formed as binaries, though some binaries later split up and leave single stars behind.[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Ngc6397_hst_blue_straggler.jpg/250px-Ngc6397_hst_blue_straggler.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ngc6397_hst_blue_straggler.jpg)

This view of [NGC 6397](/wiki/NGC_6397 "NGC 6397") includes stars known as [blue stragglers](/wiki/Blue_straggler "Blue straggler") for their location on the [Hertzsprung–Russell diagram](/wiki/Hertzsprung%E2%80%93Russell_diagram "Hertzsprung–Russell diagram").

The nearest star to the Earth, apart from the Sun, is [Proxima Centauri](/wiki/Proxima_Centauri "Proxima Centauri"), 4.2465 light-years (40.175 trillion kilometres) away. Travelling at the orbital speed of the [Space Shuttle](/wiki/Space_Shuttle "Space Shuttle"), 8 kilometres per second (29,000 kilometres per hour), it would take about 150,000 years to arrive.[[121]](#cite_note-121) This is typical of stellar separations in [galactic discs](/wiki/Disc_(galaxy) "Disc (galaxy)").[[122]](#cite_note-122) Stars can be much closer to each other in the centres of galaxies[[123]](#cite_note-123) and in globular clusters,[[124]](#cite_note-124) or much farther apart in [galactic halos](/wiki/Galactic_spheroid "Galactic spheroid").[[125]](#cite_note-125)

Due to the relatively vast distances between stars outside the galactic nucleus, collisions between stars are thought to be rare. In denser regions such as the core of globular clusters or the galactic center, collisions can be more common.[[126]](#cite_note-DarkMatter-126) Such collisions can produce what are known as [blue stragglers](/wiki/Blue_straggler "Blue straggler"). These abnormal stars have higher surface temperatures and thus are bluer than stars at the [main sequence turnoff](/wiki/Main_sequence_turnoff "Main sequence turnoff") in the cluster to which they belong; in standard stellar evolution, blue stragglers would already have evolved off the main sequence and thus would not be seen in the cluster.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

## Characteristics

Almost everything about a star is determined by its initial mass, including such characteristics as luminosity, size, evolution, lifespan, and its eventual fate.

### Age

Main article: [Stellar age estimation](/wiki/Stellar_age_estimation "Stellar age estimation")

Most stars are between 1 billion and 10 billion years old. Some stars may even be close to 13.8 billion years old—the observed [age of the universe](/wiki/Age_of_the_universe "Age of the universe"). The oldest star yet discovered, [HD 140283](/wiki/HD_140283 "HD 140283"), nicknamed Methuselah star, is an estimated 14.46 ± 0.8 billion years old.[[128]](#cite_note-Bond-140283-128) (Due to the uncertainty in the value, this age for the star does not conflict with the age of the universe, determined by the [Planck satellite](/wiki/Planck_(spacecraft) "Planck (spacecraft)") as 13.799 ± 0.021).[[128]](#cite_note-Bond-140283-128)[[129]](#cite_note-Planck_2015-129)

The more massive the star, the shorter its lifespan, primarily because massive stars have greater pressure on their cores, causing them to burn hydrogen more rapidly. The most massive stars last an average of a few million years, while stars of minimum mass (red dwarfs) burn their fuel very slowly and can last tens to hundreds of billions of years.[[130]](#cite_note-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131)
