### Chemical composition

See also: [Metallicity](/wiki/Metallicity "Metallicity") and [Molecules in stars](/wiki/Molecules_in_stars "Molecules in stars")

When stars form in the present Milky Way galaxy, they are composed of about 71% hydrogen and 27% helium,[[133]](#cite_note-133) as measured by mass, with a small fraction of heavier elements. Typically the portion of heavy elements is measured in terms of the iron content of the stellar atmosphere, as iron is a common element and its absorption lines are relatively easy to measure. The portion of heavier elements may be an indicator of the likelihood that the star has a planetary system.[[134]](#cite_note-134)

As of 2005[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Star&action=edit) the star with the lowest iron content ever measured is the dwarf HE1327-2326, with only 1/200,000th the iron content of the Sun.[[135]](#cite_note-135) By contrast, the super-metal-rich star [μ Leonis](/wiki/Mu_Leonis "Mu Leonis") has nearly double the abundance of iron as the Sun, while the planet-bearing star [14 Herculis](/wiki/14_Herculis "14 Herculis") has nearly triple the iron.[[136]](#cite_note-136) Chemically [peculiar stars](/wiki/Peculiar_star "Peculiar star") show unusual abundances of certain elements in their spectrum; especially [chromium](/wiki/Chromium "Chromium") and [rare earth elements](/wiki/Rare_earth_element "Rare earth element").[[137]](#cite_note-137) Stars with cooler outer atmospheres, including the Sun, can form various diatomic and polyatomic molecules.[[138]](#cite_note-138)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Star_Size_Comparison_2024_Update.jpg/250px-Star_Size_Comparison_2024_Update.jpg)](/wiki/File:Star_Size_Comparison_2024_Update.jpg)

Size comparison of some well-known [supergiant](/wiki/Supergiant "Supergiant") and [hypergiant](/wiki/Hypergiant "Hypergiant") stars, featuring [Cygnus OB2-12](/wiki/Cygnus_OB2-12 "Cygnus OB2-12"), [V382 Carinae](/wiki/V382_Carinae "V382 Carinae"), [Betelgeuse](/wiki/Betelgeuse "Betelgeuse"), [VV Cephei](/wiki/VV_Cephei "VV Cephei"), and [VY Canis Majoris](/wiki/VY_Canis_Majoris "VY Canis Majoris")

### Diameter

Main articles: [List of largest stars](/wiki/List_of_largest_stars "List of largest stars"), [List of smallest stars](/wiki/List_of_smallest_stars "List of smallest stars"), and [Solar radius](/wiki/Solar_radius "Solar radius")

Due to their great distance from the Earth, all stars except the Sun appear to the unaided eye as shining points in the night sky that [twinkle](/wiki/Twinkling "Twinkling") because of the effect of the Earth's atmosphere. The Sun is close enough to the Earth to appear as a disk instead, and to provide daylight. Other than the Sun, the star with the largest apparent size is [R Doradus](/wiki/R_Doradus "R Doradus"), with an [angular diameter](/wiki/Angular_diameter "Angular diameter") of only 0.057 [arcseconds](/wiki/Arcsecond "Arcsecond").[[139]](#cite_note-139)

The disks of most stars are much too small in [angular size](/wiki/Angular_diameter "Angular diameter") to be observed with current ground-based optical telescopes, so [interferometer](/wiki/Interferometry "Interferometry") telescopes are required to produce images of these objects. Another technique for measuring the angular size of stars is through [occultation](/wiki/Occultation "Occultation"). By precisely measuring the drop in brightness of a star as it is occulted by the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") (or the rise in brightness when it reappears), the star's angular diameter can be computed.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

Stars range in size from neutron stars, which vary anywhere from 20 to 40 km (25 mi) in diameter, to [supergiants](/wiki/Supergiant "Supergiant") like [Betelgeuse](/wiki/Betelgeuse "Betelgeuse") in the [Orion constellation](/wiki/Orion_constellation "Orion constellation"), which has a diameter about 640 times that of the Sun[[141]](#cite_note-141) with a much lower [density](/wiki/Density "Density").[[142]](#cite_note-142)

### Kinematics

Main article: [Stellar kinematics](/wiki/Stellar_kinematics "Stellar kinematics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Pleiades_large.jpg/250px-Pleiades_large.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pleiades_large.jpg)

The [Pleiades](/wiki/Pleiades "Pleiades"), an [open cluster](/wiki/Open_cluster "Open cluster") of stars in the [constellation](/wiki/Constellation "Constellation") of [Taurus](/wiki/Taurus_(constellation) "Taurus (constellation)"). These stars share a common motion through space.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

The motion of a star relative to the Sun can provide useful information about the origin and age of a star, as well as the structure and evolution of the surrounding galaxy.[[144]](#cite_note-144) The components of motion of a star consist of the [radial velocity](/wiki/Radial_velocity "Radial velocity") toward or away from the Sun, and the traverse angular movement, which is called its proper motion.[[145]](#cite_note-145)

Radial velocity is measured by the [doppler shift](/wiki/Doppler_shift "Doppler shift") of the star's spectral lines and is given in units of km/[s](/wiki/Second "Second"). The proper motion of a star, its parallax, is determined by precise astrometric measurements in units of milli-[arc seconds](/wiki/Arc_second "Arc second") (mas) per year. With knowledge of the star's parallax and its distance, the proper motion velocity can be calculated. Together with the radial velocity, the total velocity can be calculated. Stars with high rates of proper motion are likely to be relatively close to the Sun, making them good candidates for parallax measurements.[[146]](#cite_note-146)

When both rates of movement are known, the [space velocity](/wiki/Space_velocity_(astronomy) "Space velocity (astronomy)") of the star relative to the Sun or the galaxy can be computed. Among nearby stars, it has been found that younger population I stars have generally lower velocities than older, population II stars. The latter have elliptical orbits that are inclined to the plane of the galaxy.[[147]](#cite_note-147) A comparison of the kinematics of nearby stars has allowed astronomers to trace their origin to common points in giant molecular clouds; such groups with common points of origin are referred to as [stellar associations](/wiki/Stellar_association "Stellar association").[[148]](#cite_note-148)

### Magnetic field

Main article: [Stellar magnetic field](/wiki/Stellar_magnetic_field "Stellar magnetic field")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Suaur.jpg/250px-Suaur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suaur.jpg)

Surface magnetic field of [SU Aur](/wiki/SU_Aurigae "SU Aurigae") (a young star of [T Tauri type](/wiki/T_Tauri_star "T Tauri star")), reconstructed by means of [Zeeman–Doppler imaging](/wiki/Zeeman%E2%80%93Doppler_imaging "Zeeman–Doppler imaging")

The [magnetic field](/wiki/Magnetic_field "Magnetic field") of a star is generated within regions of the interior where convective circulation occurs. This movement of conductive plasma functions like a [dynamo](/wiki/Dynamo_theory "Dynamo theory"), wherein the movement of electrical charges induce magnetic fields, as does a mechanical dynamo. Those magnetic fields have a great range that extend throughout and beyond the star. The strength of the magnetic field varies with the mass and composition of the star, and the amount of magnetic surface activity depends upon the star's rate of rotation. This surface activity produces [starspots](/wiki/Starspot "Starspot"), which are regions of strong magnetic fields and lower than normal surface temperatures. [Coronal loops](/wiki/Coronal_loop "Coronal loop") are arching magnetic field flux lines that rise from a star's surface into the star's outer atmosphere, its corona. The coronal loops can be seen due to the plasma they conduct along their length. [Stellar flares](/wiki/Stellar_flare "Stellar flare") are bursts of high-energy particles that are emitted due to the same magnetic activity.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

Young, rapidly rotating stars tend to have high levels of surface activity because of their magnetic field. The magnetic field can act upon a star's stellar wind, functioning as a brake to gradually slow the rate of rotation with time. Thus, older stars such as the Sun have a much slower rate of rotation and a lower level of surface activity. The activity levels of slowly rotating stars tend to vary in a cyclical manner and can shut down altogether for periods of time.[[150]](#cite_note-Michelson_Starspots-150) During the [Maunder Minimum](/wiki/Maunder_Minimum "Maunder Minimum"), for example, the Sun underwent a 70-year period with almost no sunspot activity.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

### Mass

Main article: [Stellar mass](/wiki/Stellar_mass "Stellar mass")

Stars have masses ranging from less than half the solar mass to over 200 solar masses (see [List of most massive stars](/wiki/List_of_most_massive_stars "List of most massive stars")). One of the most massive stars known is [Eta Carinae](/wiki/Eta_Carinae "Eta Carinae"),[[152]](#cite_note-152) which, with 100–150 times as much mass as the Sun, will have a lifespan of only several million years. Studies of the most massive open clusters suggests 150 M☉ as a rough upper limit for stars in the current era of the universe.[[153]](#cite_note-weidner-153) This represents an empirical value for the theoretical limit on the mass of forming stars due to increasing radiation pressure on the accreting gas cloud. Several stars in the [R136](/wiki/R136 "R136") cluster in the [Large Magellanic Cloud](/wiki/Large_Magellanic_Cloud "Large Magellanic Cloud") have been measured with larger masses,[[154]](#cite_note-hainich-154) but it has been determined that they could have been created through the collision and merger of massive stars in close binary systems, sidestepping the 150 M☉ limit on massive star formation.[[155]](#cite_note-banerjee-155)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Ngc1999.jpg/250px-Ngc1999.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ngc1999.jpg)

The [reflection nebula](/wiki/Reflection_nebula "Reflection nebula") [NGC 1999](/wiki/NGC_1999 "NGC 1999") is brilliantly illuminated by [V380 Orionis](/wiki/V380_Orionis "V380 Orionis"). The black patch of sky is a vast hole of empty space and not a [dark nebula](/wiki/Dark_nebula "Dark nebula") as previously thought.

The first stars to form after the Big Bang may have been larger, up to 300 M☉,[[156]](#cite_note-156) due to the complete absence of elements heavier than [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium") in their composition. This generation of supermassive [population III stars](/wiki/Population_III_stars "Population III stars") is likely to have existed in the very early universe (i.e., they are observed to have a high redshift), and may have started the production of chemical elements heavier than [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") that are needed for the later formation of planets and life. In June 2015, astronomers reported evidence for Population III stars in the [Cosmos Redshift 7](/wiki/Cosmos_Redshift_7 "Cosmos Redshift 7") galaxy at *z* = 6.60.[[157]](#cite_note-AJ-20150604-157)[[158]](#cite_note-NYT-20150617-158)

With a mass only 80 times that of Jupiter (MJ), [2MASS J0523-1403](/wiki/2MASS_J0523-1403 "2MASS J0523-1403") is the smallest known star undergoing nuclear fusion in its core.[[159]](#cite_note-SIMBAD-159) For stars with metallicity similar to the Sun, the theoretical minimum mass the star can have and still undergo fusion at the core, is estimated to be about 75 MJ.[[160]](#cite_note-boss_planets_or_what-160)[[161]](#cite_note-minimum-161) When the metallicity is very low, the minimum star size seems to be about 8.3% of the solar mass, or about 87 MJ.[[161]](#cite_note-minimum-161)[[162]](#cite_note-162) Smaller bodies called [brown dwarfs](/wiki/Brown_dwarf "Brown dwarf"), occupy a poorly defined grey area between stars and [gas giants](/wiki/Gas_giant "Gas giant").[[160]](#cite_note-boss_planets_or_what-160)[[161]](#cite_note-minimum-161)

The combination of the radius and the mass of a star determines its surface gravity. Giant stars have much lower surface gravity than do main-sequence stars, while the opposite is the case for degenerate, compact stars such as white dwarfs. The surface gravity can influence the appearance of a star's spectrum, with higher gravity causing a broadening of the [absorption lines](/wiki/Absorption_line "Absorption line").[[36]](#cite_note-new_cosmos-36)

### Rotation

Main article: [Stellar rotation](/wiki/Stellar_rotation "Stellar rotation")

The rotation rate of stars can be determined through [spectroscopic measurement](/wiki/Spectroscopy "Spectroscopy"), or more exactly determined by tracking their [starspots](/wiki/Starspot "Starspot"). Young stars can have a rotation greater than 100 km/s at the equator. The B-class star [Achernar](/wiki/Achernar "Achernar"), for example, has an equatorial velocity of about 225 km/s or greater, causing its [equator to bulge outward](/wiki/Equatorial_bulge "Equatorial bulge") and giving it an equatorial diameter that is more than 50% greater than between the poles. This rate of rotation is just below the critical velocity of 300 km/s at which speed the star would break apart.[[163]](#cite_note-163) By contrast, the Sun rotates once every 25–35 days depending on latitude,[[164]](#cite_note-164) with an equatorial velocity of 1.93 km/s.[[165]](#cite_note-165) A main-sequence star's magnetic field and the stellar wind serve to slow its rotation by a significant amount as it evolves on the main sequence.[[166]](#cite_note-166)

[Degenerate stars](/wiki/Degenerate_star "Degenerate star") have contracted into a compact mass, resulting in a rapid rate of rotation. However they have relatively low rates of rotation compared to what would be expected by conservation of angular momentum—the tendency of a rotating body to compensate for a contraction in size by increasing its rate of spin. A large portion of the star's angular momentum is dissipated as a result of mass loss through the stellar wind.[[167]](#cite_note-167) In spite of this, the rate of rotation for a pulsar can be very rapid. The pulsar at the heart of the [Crab nebula](/wiki/Crab_nebula "Crab nebula"), for example, rotates 30 times per second.[[168]](#cite_note-168) The rotation rate of the pulsar will gradually slow due to the emission of radiation.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

### Temperature

The surface temperature of a main-sequence star is determined by the rate of energy production of its core and by its radius, and is often estimated from the star's [color index](/wiki/Color_index "Color index").[[170]](#cite_note-astronomynotes-170) The temperature is normally given in terms of an [effective temperature](/wiki/Effective_temperature "Effective temperature"), which is the temperature of an idealized black body that radiates its energy at the same luminosity per surface area as the star. The effective temperature is only representative of the surface, as the temperature increases toward the core.[[171]](#cite_note-171) The temperature in the core region of a star is several million [kelvins](/wiki/Kelvin "Kelvin").[[172]](#cite_note-aps_mss-172)

The stellar temperature will determine the rate of ionization of various elements, resulting in characteristic absorption lines in the spectrum. The surface temperature of a star, along with its visual [absolute magnitude](/wiki/Absolute_magnitude "Absolute magnitude") and absorption features, is used to classify a star (see classification below).[[36]](#cite_note-new_cosmos-36)

Massive main-sequence stars can have surface temperatures of 50,000 K. Smaller stars such as the Sun have surface temperatures of a few thousand K. Red giants have relatively low surface temperatures of about 3,600 K; but they have a high luminosity due to their large exterior surface area.[[173]](#cite_note-zeilik-173)

## Radiation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Eta_Carinae.jpg/330px-Eta_Carinae.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eta_Carinae.jpg)

[Eta Carinae](/wiki/Eta_Carinae "Eta Carinae") is an unstable [blue hypergiant](/wiki/Blue_hypergiant "Blue hypergiant") star, roughly 100 times more massive than the Sun, over 700 times wider, and 4 million times more luminous. In a 19th century event termed the Great Eruption, Eta Carinae brightened and violently ejected mass to form the surrounding [Homunculus Nebula](/wiki/Homunculus_Nebula "Homunculus Nebula") (pictured).

The energy produced by stars, a product of nuclear fusion, radiates to space as both [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") and [particle radiation](/wiki/Particle_radiation "Particle radiation"). The particle radiation emitted by a star is manifested as the stellar wind,[[174]](#cite_note-174) which streams from the outer layers as electrically charged [protons](/wiki/Proton "Proton") and [alpha](/wiki/Alpha_particle "Alpha particle") and [beta particles](/wiki/Beta_particle "Beta particle"). A steady stream of almost massless neutrinos emanate directly from the star's core.[[175]](#cite_note-carroll_ostlie_ch11-175)

The production of energy at the core is the reason stars shine so brightly: every time two or more atomic nuclei fuse together to form a single [atomic nucleus](/wiki/Atomic_nucleus "Atomic nucleus") of a new heavier element, [gamma ray](/wiki/Gamma_ray "Gamma ray") [photons](/wiki/Photon "Photon") are released from the nuclear fusion product. This energy is converted to other forms of [electromagnetic energy](/wiki/Electromagnetic_energy "Electromagnetic energy") of lower frequency, such as visible light, by the time it reaches the star's outer layers.[[176]](#cite_note-carroll_ostlie_ch10-176)

The color of a star, as determined by the most intense [frequency](/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") of the visible light, depends on the temperature of the star's outer layers, including its [photosphere](/wiki/Photosphere "Photosphere").[[177]](#cite_note-177) Besides visible light, stars emit forms of electromagnetic radiation that are invisible to the [human eye](/wiki/Human_eye "Human eye"). In fact, stellar electromagnetic radiation spans the entire [electromagnetic spectrum](/wiki/Electromagnetic_spectrum "Electromagnetic spectrum"), from the longest [wavelengths](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength") of [radio waves](/wiki/Radio_frequency "Radio frequency") through [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared"), visible light, [ultraviolet](/wiki/Ultraviolet "Ultraviolet"), to the shortest of [X-rays](/wiki/X-ray "X-ray"), and gamma rays. From the standpoint of total energy emitted by a star, not all components of stellar electromagnetic radiation are significant, but all frequencies provide insight into the star's physics.[[175]](#cite_note-carroll_ostlie_ch11-175)

Using the [stellar spectrum](/wiki/Stellar_spectrum "Stellar spectrum"), astronomers can determine the surface temperature, [surface gravity](/wiki/Surface_gravity "Surface gravity"), metallicity and [rotational](/wiki/Rotation "Rotation") velocity of a star. If the distance of the star is found, such as by measuring the parallax, then the luminosity of the star can be derived. The mass, radius, surface gravity, and rotation period can then be estimated based on stellar models. (Mass can be calculated for stars in [binary systems](/wiki/Binary_star "Binary star") by measuring their orbital velocities and distances. [Gravitational microlensing](/wiki/Gravitational_microlensing "Gravitational microlensing") has been used to measure the mass of a single star.[[178]](#cite_note-178)) With these parameters, astronomers can estimate the age of the star.[[179]](#cite_note-179)

### Luminosity

The luminosity of a star is the amount of light and other forms of [radiant energy](/wiki/Radiant_energy "Radiant energy") it radiates per unit of time. It has units of [power](/wiki/Power_(physics) "Power (physics)"). The luminosity of a star is determined by its radius and surface temperature. Many stars do not radiate uniformly across their entire surface. The rapidly rotating star [Vega](/wiki/Vega "Vega"), for example, has a higher [energy flux](/wiki/Energy_flux "Energy flux") (power per unit area) at its poles than along its equator.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

Patches of the star's surface with a lower temperature and luminosity than average are known as [starspots](/wiki/Sunspot "Sunspot"). Small, *dwarf* stars such as the Sun generally have essentially featureless disks with only small starspots. *Giant* stars have much larger, more obvious starspots,[[150]](#cite_note-Michelson_Starspots-150) and they exhibit strong stellar [limb darkening](/wiki/Limb_darkening "Limb darkening"). That is, the brightness decreases towards the edge of the stellar disk.[[181]](#cite_note-181) Red dwarf [flare stars](/wiki/Flare_star "Flare star") such as [UV Ceti](/wiki/UV_Ceti "UV Ceti") may possess prominent starspot features.[[182]](#cite_note-182)

### Magnitude

Main articles: [Apparent magnitude](/wiki/Apparent_magnitude "Apparent magnitude") and [Absolute magnitude](/wiki/Absolute_magnitude "Absolute magnitude")

The apparent [brightness](/wiki/Brightness "Brightness") of a star is expressed in terms of its [apparent magnitude](/wiki/Apparent_magnitude "Apparent magnitude"). It is a function of the star's luminosity, its distance from Earth, the [extinction](/wiki/Extinction_(astronomy) "Extinction (astronomy)") effect of [interstellar dust](/wiki/Interstellar_dust "Interstellar dust") and gas, and the altering of the star's light as it passes through Earth's atmosphere. Intrinsic or absolute magnitude is directly related to a star's luminosity, and is the apparent magnitude a star would be if the distance between the Earth and the star were 10 parsecs (32.6 light-years).[[183]](#cite_note-Lawrence2019-183)

Number of stars brighter than magnitude

| Apparent magnitude | Number  of stars[[184]](#cite_note-184) |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | 4 |
| 1 | 15 |
| 2 | 48 |
| 3 | 171 |
| 4 | 513 |
| 5 | 1,602 |
| 6 | 4,800 |
| 7 | 14,000 |

Both the apparent and absolute magnitude scales are [logarithmic units](/wiki/Logarithmic_units "Logarithmic units"): one whole number difference in magnitude is equal to a brightness variation of about 2.5 times[[185]](#cite_note-luminosity-185) (the [5th root](/wiki/Nth_root "Nth root") of 100 or approximately 2.512). This means that a [first magnitude star](/wiki/First_magnitude_star "First magnitude star") (+1.00) is about 2.5 times brighter than a [second magnitude](/wiki/Second_magnitude_star "Second magnitude star") (+2.00) star, and about 100 times brighter than a [sixth magnitude star](/wiki/Sixth_magnitude_star "Sixth magnitude star") (+6.00). The faintest stars visible to the naked eye under good seeing conditions are about magnitude +6.[[186]](#cite_note-Nicolson1999-186)

On both apparent and absolute magnitude scales, the smaller the magnitude number, the brighter the star; the larger the magnitude number, the fainter the star. The brightest stars, on either scale, have negative magnitude numbers. The variation in brightness (Δ*L*) between two stars is calculated by subtracting the magnitude number of the brighter star (*m*b) from the magnitude number of the fainter star (*m*f), then using the difference as an exponent for the base number 2.512; that is to say:

:   ![{\displaystyle \Delta {m}=m_{\mathrm {f} }-m_{\mathrm {b} }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/925079954c7da1d32aafb1ad12c3acef894a36f1)
:   ![{\displaystyle 2.512^{\Delta {m}}=\Delta {L}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/55e0f7f561c14a3c1becb08a29fa6b4dc8c10392)

Relative to both luminosity and distance from Earth, a star's absolute magnitude (*M*) and apparent magnitude (*m*) are not equivalent;[[185]](#cite_note-luminosity-185) for example, the bright star Sirius has an apparent magnitude of −1.44, but it has an absolute magnitude of +1.41.

The Sun has an apparent magnitude of −26.7, but its absolute magnitude is only +4.83. Sirius, the brightest star in the night sky as seen from Earth, is approximately 23 times more luminous than the Sun, while [Canopus](/wiki/Canopus "Canopus"), the second brightest star in the night sky with an absolute magnitude of −5.53, is approximately 14,000 times more luminous than the Sun. Despite Canopus being vastly more luminous than Sirius, the latter star appears the brighter of the two. This is because Sirius is merely 8.6 light-years from the Earth, while Canopus is much farther away at a distance of 310 light-years.[[187]](#cite_note-187)

The [most luminous known stars](/wiki/List_of_most_luminous_stars "List of most luminous stars") have absolute magnitudes of roughly −12, corresponding to 6 million times the luminosity of the Sun.[[188]](#cite_note-188) Theoretically, the least luminous stars are at the lower limit of mass at which stars are capable of supporting nuclear fusion of hydrogen in the core; stars just above this limit have been located in the [NGC 6397](/wiki/NGC_6397 "NGC 6397") cluster. The faintest red dwarfs in the cluster are absolute magnitude 15, while a 17th absolute magnitude white dwarf has been discovered.[[189]](#cite_note-189)[[190]](#cite_note-190)

## Classification

Main article: [Stellar classification](/wiki/Stellar_classification "Stellar classification")

Surface temperature ranges for  
different stellar classes[[191]](#cite_note-191)

| Class | Temperature | Sample star |
| --- | --- | --- |
| O | 33,000 K or more | [Zeta Ophiuchi](/wiki/Zeta_Ophiuchi "Zeta Ophiuchi") |
| B | 10,500–30,000 K | [Rigel](/wiki/Rigel "Rigel") |
| A | 7,500–10,000 K | [Altair](/wiki/Altair "Altair") |
| F | 6,000–7,200 K | [Procyon A](/wiki/Procyon "Procyon") |
| G | 5,500–6,000 K | [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") |
| K | 4,000–5,250 K | [Epsilon Indi](/wiki/Epsilon_Indi "Epsilon Indi") |
| M | 2,600–3,850 K | [Proxima Centauri](/wiki/Proxima_Centauri "Proxima Centauri") |

The current stellar classification system originated in the early 20th century, when stars were classified from *A* to *Q* based on the strength of the [hydrogen line](/wiki/Hydrogen_line "Hydrogen line").[[192]](#cite_note-192) It was thought that the hydrogen line strength was a simple linear function of temperature. Instead, it was more complicated: it strengthened with increasing temperature, peaked near 9000 K, and then declined at greater temperatures. The classifications were since reordered by temperature, on which the modern scheme is based.[[193]](#cite_note-carlos-193)

Stars are given a single-letter classification according to their spectra, ranging from type *O*, which are very hot, to *M*, which are so cool that molecules may form in their atmospheres. The main classifications in order of decreasing surface temperature are: *O, B, A, F, G, K*, and *M*. A variety of rare spectral types are given special classifications. The most common of these are types *L* and *T*, which classify the coldest low-mass stars and brown dwarfs. Each letter has 10 sub-divisions, numbered from 0 to 9, in order of decreasing temperature. However, this system breaks down at extreme high temperatures as classes *O0* and *O1* may not exist.[[194]](#cite_note-spectrum-194)

In addition, stars may be classified by the luminosity effects found in their spectral lines, which correspond to their spatial size and is determined by their surface gravity. These range from *0* ([hypergiants](/wiki/Hypergiant "Hypergiant")) through *III* ([giants](/wiki/Giant_star "Giant star")) to *V* (main-sequence dwarfs); some authors add *VII* (white dwarfs). Main-sequence stars fall along a narrow, diagonal band when graphed according to their absolute magnitude and spectral type.[[194]](#cite_note-spectrum-194) The Sun is a main-sequence *G2V* yellow dwarf of intermediate temperature and ordinary size.[[195]](#cite_note-RixHay2015-195)

There is additional nomenclature in the form of lower-case letters added to the end of the spectral type to indicate peculiar features of the spectrum. For example, an "*e*" can indicate the presence of emission lines; "*m*" represents unusually strong levels of metals, and "*var*" can mean variations in the spectral type.[[194]](#cite_note-spectrum-194)

White dwarf stars have their own class that begins with the letter *D*. This is further sub-divided into the classes *DA*, *DB*, *DC*, *DO*, *DZ*, and *DQ*, depending on the types of prominent lines found in the spectrum. This is followed by a numerical value that indicates the temperature.[[196]](#cite_note-196)

## Variable stars

Main article: [Variable star](/wiki/Variable_star "Variable star")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Mira_1997.jpg/250px-Mira_1997.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mira_1997.jpg)

[Mira](/wiki/Mira "Mira"), an oscillating variable star on the asymptotic giant branch, is a red giant nearing the end of its life, noted for its asymmetrical appearance.

Variable stars have periodic or random changes in luminosity because of intrinsic or extrinsic properties. Of the intrinsically variable stars, the primary types can be subdivided into three principal groups.

During their stellar evolution, some stars pass through phases where they can become pulsating variables. Pulsating variable stars vary in radius and luminosity over time, expanding and contracting with periods ranging from minutes to years, depending on the size of the star. This category includes [Cepheid and Cepheid-like stars](/wiki/Cepheid_variable "Cepheid variable"), and long-period variables such as [Mira](/wiki/Mira_variable "Mira variable").[[197]](#cite_note-variables-197)

Eruptive variables are stars that experience sudden increases in luminosity because of flares or mass ejection events.[[197]](#cite_note-variables-197) This group includes protostars, Wolf-Rayet stars, and flare stars, as well as giant and supergiant stars.

Cataclysmic or explosive variable stars are those that undergo a dramatic change in their properties. This group includes [novae](/wiki/Nova "Nova") and supernovae. A binary star system that includes a nearby white dwarf can produce certain types of these spectacular stellar explosions, including the nova and a Type Ia supernova.[[89]](#cite_note-iben-89) The explosion is created when the white dwarf accretes hydrogen from the companion star, building up mass until the hydrogen undergoes fusion.[[198]](#cite_note-198) Some novae are recurrent, having periodic outbursts of moderate amplitude.[[197]](#cite_note-variables-197)

Stars can vary in luminosity because of extrinsic factors, such as eclipsing binaries, as well as rotating stars that produce extreme starspots.[[197]](#cite_note-variables-197) A notable example of an eclipsing binary is Algol, which regularly varies in magnitude from 2.1 to 3.4 over a period of 2.87 days.[[199]](#cite_note-gcvs-199)

## Structure

Main article: [Stellar structure](/wiki/Stellar_structure "Stellar structure")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Star_types.svg/500px-Star_types.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Star_types.svg)

Internal structures of main-sequence stars with masses indicated in solar masses, convection zones with arrowed cycles, and radiative zones with red flashes. Left to right, a [red dwarf](/wiki/Red_dwarf "Red dwarf"), a [yellow dwarf](/wiki/Yellow_dwarf_star "Yellow dwarf star"), and a [blue-white main-sequence star](/wiki/Stellar_classification "Stellar classification")

The interior of a stable star is in a state of [hydrostatic equilibrium](/wiki/Hydrostatic_equilibrium "Hydrostatic equilibrium"): the forces on any small volume almost exactly counterbalance each other. The balanced forces are inward gravitational force and an outward force due to the pressure [gradient](/wiki/Gradient "Gradient") within the star. The [pressure gradient](/wiki/Pressure_gradient "Pressure gradient") is established by the temperature gradient of the plasma; the outer part of the star is cooler than the core. The temperature at the core of a main-sequence or giant star is at least on the order of 107 K. The resulting temperature and pressure at the hydrogen-burning core of a main-sequence star are sufficient for [nuclear fusion](/wiki/Nuclear_fusion "Nuclear fusion") to occur and for sufficient energy to be produced to prevent further collapse of the star.[[200]](#cite_note-hansen-200)[[201]](#cite_note-Schwarzschild-201)

As atomic nuclei are fused in the core, they emit energy in the form of gamma rays. These photons interact with the surrounding plasma, adding to the thermal energy at the core. Stars on the main sequence convert hydrogen into helium, creating a slowly but steadily increasing proportion of helium in the core. Eventually the helium content becomes predominant, and energy production ceases at the core. Instead, for stars of more than 0.4 M☉, fusion occurs in a slowly expanding shell around the [degenerate](/wiki/Degenerate_matter "Degenerate matter") helium core.[[202]](#cite_note-202)

In addition to hydrostatic equilibrium, the interior of a stable star will maintain an energy balance of [thermal equilibrium](/wiki/Thermal_equilibrium "Thermal equilibrium"). There is a radial temperature gradient throughout the interior that results in a flux of energy flowing toward the exterior. The outgoing flux of energy leaving any layer within the star will exactly match the incoming flux from below.[[203]](#cite_note-HuangYu1998-203)

The [radiation zone](/wiki/Radiation_zone "Radiation zone") is the region of the stellar interior where the flux of energy outward is dependent on radiative heat transfer, since convective heat transfer is inefficient in that zone. In this region the plasma will not be perturbed, and any mass motions will die out. Where this is not the case, then the plasma becomes unstable and convection will occur, forming a [convection zone](/wiki/Convection_zone "Convection zone"). This can occur, for example, in regions where very high energy fluxes occur, such as near the core or in areas with high [opacity](/wiki/Opacity_(optics) "Opacity (optics)") (making radiatative heat transfer inefficient) as in the outer envelope.[[201]](#cite_note-Schwarzschild-201)

The occurrence of convection in the outer envelope of a main-sequence star depends on the star's mass. Stars with several times the mass of the Sun have a convection zone deep within the interior and a radiative zone in the outer layers. Smaller stars such as the Sun are just the opposite, with the convective zone located in the outer layers.[[204]](#cite_note-imagine-204) Red dwarf stars with less than 0.4 M☉ are convective throughout, which prevents the accumulation of a helium core.[[86]](#cite_note-late_stages-86) For most stars the convective zones will vary over time as the star ages and the constitution of the interior is modified.[[201]](#cite_note-Schwarzschild-201)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/da/Sun_parts_big.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sun_parts_big.jpg)

A cross-section of the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun")

The photosphere is that portion of a star that is visible to an observer. This is the layer at which the plasma of the star becomes transparent to photons of light. From here, the energy generated at the core becomes free to propagate into space. It is within the photosphere that [sun spots](/wiki/Sun_spots "Sun spots"), regions of lower than average temperature, appear.[[205]](#cite_note-NewcombHolden1887-205)

Above the level of the photosphere is the stellar atmosphere. In a main-sequence star such as the Sun, the lowest level of the atmosphere, just above the photosphere, is the thin [chromosphere](/wiki/Chromosphere "Chromosphere") region, where [spicules](/wiki/Solar_spicule "Solar spicule") appear and [stellar flares](/wiki/Solar_flare "Solar flare") begin. Above this is the transition region, where the temperature rapidly increases within a distance of only 100 km (62 mi). Beyond this is the [corona](/wiki/Stellar_corona "Stellar corona"), a volume of super-heated plasma that can extend outward to several million kilometres.[[206]](#cite_note-206) The existence of a corona appears to be dependent on a convective zone in the outer layers of the star.[[204]](#cite_note-imagine-204) Despite its high temperature, the corona emits very little light, due to its low gas density.[[207]](#cite_note-207) The corona region of the Sun is normally only visible during a [solar eclipse](/wiki/Solar_eclipse "Solar eclipse").

From the corona, a stellar wind of plasma particles expands outward from the star, until it interacts with the interstellar medium. For the Sun, the influence of its [solar wind](/wiki/Solar_wind "Solar wind") extends throughout a bubble-shaped region called the [heliosphere](/wiki/Heliosphere "Heliosphere").[[208]](#cite_note-208)

## Nuclear fusion reaction pathways

Main article: [Stellar nucleosynthesis](/wiki/Stellar_nucleosynthesis "Stellar nucleosynthesis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Fusion_in_the_Sun.svg/250px-Fusion_in_the_Sun.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Fusion_in_the_Sun.svg)

Overview of the proton–proton chain

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/CNO_Cycle.svg/250px-CNO_Cycle.svg.png)](/wiki/File:CNO_Cycle.svg)

The carbon-nitrogen-oxygen cycle

When nuclei fuse, the mass of the fused product is less than the mass of the original parts. This lost mass is converted to electromagnetic energy, according to the [mass–energy equivalence](/wiki/Mass%E2%80%93energy_equivalence "Mass–energy equivalence") relationship ![{\displaystyle E=mc^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9f73dbd37a0cac34406ee89057fa1b36a1e6a18e).[[209]](#cite_note-sunshine-209) A variety of nuclear fusion reactions take place in the cores of stars, that depend upon their mass and composition.

The hydrogen fusion process is temperature-sensitive, so a moderate increase in the core temperature will result in a significant increase in the fusion rate. As a result, the core temperature of main-sequence stars only varies from 4 million kelvin for a small M-class star to 40 million kelvin for a massive O-class star.[[172]](#cite_note-aps_mss-172)

In the Sun, with a 16-million-kelvin core, hydrogen fuses to form helium in the [proton–proton chain reaction](/wiki/Proton%E2%80%93proton_chain_reaction "Proton–proton chain reaction"):[[210]](#cite_note-synthesis-210)

:   4[1H](/wiki/Hydrogen-1 "Hydrogen-1") → 2[2H](/wiki/Deuterium "Deuterium") + 2[e+](/wiki/Positron "Positron") + 2[νe](/wiki/Neutrino "Neutrino")(2 x 0.4 M[eV](/wiki/Electronvolt "Electronvolt"))
:   2[e+](/wiki/Positron "Positron") + 2[e](/wiki/Positron "Positron")− → 2[γ](/wiki/Photon "Photon") (2 x 1.0 MeV)
:   21H + 22H → 2[3He](/wiki/Helium-3 "Helium-3") + 2[γ](/wiki/Photon "Photon") (2 x 5.5 MeV)
:   23He → [4He](/wiki/Helium-4 "Helium-4") + 21H (12.9 MeV)

There are a couple other paths, in which 3He and 4He combine to form 7Be, which eventually (with the addition of another proton) yields two 4He, a gain of one.

All these reactions result in the overall reaction:

:   41H → 4He + 2γ + 2νe (26.7 MeV)

where γ is a gamma ray photon, νe is a neutrino, and H and He are isotopes of hydrogen and helium, respectively. The energy released by this reaction is in millions of electron volts. Each individual reaction produces only a tiny amount of energy, but because enormous numbers of these reactions occur constantly, they produce all the energy necessary to sustain the star's radiation output. In comparison, the combustion of two hydrogen gas molecules with one oxygen gas molecule releases only 5.7 eV.

In more massive stars, helium is produced in a cycle of reactions [catalyzed](/wiki/Catalyst "Catalyst") by carbon called the [carbon-nitrogen-oxygen cycle](/wiki/CNO_cycle "CNO cycle").[[210]](#cite_note-synthesis-210)

In evolved stars with cores at 100 million kelvin and masses between 0.5 and 10 M☉, helium can be transformed into carbon in the [triple-alpha process](/wiki/Triple-alpha_process "Triple-alpha process") that uses the intermediate element [beryllium](/wiki/Beryllium "Beryllium"):[[210]](#cite_note-synthesis-210)

:   4He + 4He + 92 keV → [8\*Be](/wiki/Isotopes_of_beryllium "Isotopes of beryllium")
:   4He + 8\*Be + 67 keV → 12\*C
:   12\*C → [12C](/wiki/Carbon-12 "Carbon-12") + γ + 7.4 MeV

For an overall reaction of:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Kernfusionen0_en.png/500px-Kernfusionen0_en.png)](/wiki/File:Kernfusionen0_en.png)

Overview of consecutive fusion processes in massive stars

:   34He → 12C + γ + 7.2 MeV

In massive stars, heavier elements can be burned in a contracting core through the [neon-burning process](/wiki/Neon-burning_process "Neon-burning process") and [oxygen-burning process](/wiki/Oxygen-burning_process "Oxygen-burning process"). The final stage in the stellar nucleosynthesis process is the [silicon-burning process](/wiki/Silicon-burning_process "Silicon-burning process") that results in the production of the stable isotope iron-56.[[210]](#cite_note-synthesis-210) Any further fusion would be an endothermic process that consumes energy, and so further energy can only be produced through gravitational collapse.

Duration of the main phases of fusion for a 20 M☉ star[[211]](#cite_note-211)

| Fuel material | Temperature (million kelvins) | Density (kg/cm3) | Burn duration (τ in years) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| H | 37 | 0.0045 | 8.1 million |
| He | 188 | 0.97 | 1.2 million |
| C | 870 | 170 | 976 |
| Ne | 1,570 | 3,100 | 0.6 |
| O | 1,980 | 5,550 | 1.25 |
| S/Si | 3,340 | 33,400 | 0.0315 (~11.5 days) |
